name: Python
scope: An individual at BASIC level, with 0-2 years of experience, understands Python syntax and basic programming concepts like variables, data types (integers, floats, strings, booleans), and operators (arithmetic, comparison, logical). They can use control flow statements (if, elif, else) and loops (for, while) to solve simple problems. They are capable of writing simple scripts to automate basic tasks, perform calculations, and handle file I/O operations like reading from and writing to text files. They can work with fundamental data structures such as lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets, and can define and call functions with arguments and return values. They know how to import and use standard library modules (e.g., math, random, datetime) and handle exceptions using try and except blocks. They use basic tools like text editors or simple IDEs (IDLE, Visual Studio Code), run scripts from the command line, and manage packages with pip. They can debug simple syntax and runtime errors using print statements, read and understand straightforward code written by others, and make minor edits to existing scripts under guidance. They are not expected to design complex systems but can follow predefined specifications and contribute to projects by handling straightforward tasks.

name: SQL
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in SQL as a competency should be able to / is capable of creating and altering tables with fundamental data types and constraints (e.g., PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK) using DDL statements, performing routine CRUD operations with confidence through DML statements, and applying simple normalization principles (1NF, 2NF, 3NF) to reduce redundancy. They should confidently write SELECT queries leveraging WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, and basic JOINs (INNER, LEFT) while also handling simple subqueries embedded in WHERE clauses or SELECT statements. They can use common SQL tools like MySQL Workbench, SQL Server Management Studio, or pgAdmin to execute queries, explore schema objects, and perform basic imports or exports in CSV or SQL dump formats. They should understand the concept of transactions, using BEGIN, COMMIT, or ROLLBACK to maintain data consistency, while having a rudimentary awareness of locks and isolation levels. They should also recognize how indexes can improve query performance, read simple execution plans to avoid obvious bottlenecks, and apply foundational optimization techniques such as selecting only needed columns. They should grasp the importance of constraints in maintaining referential integrity, be able to store and retrieve SQL scripts in version control systems like Git, and know the basics of granting or revoking privileges to maintain simple role-based permissions. They should demonstrate basic communication and collaboration skills, discussing requirements and data definitions with peers, while documenting queries and schemas to ensure clarity. They should know how to troubleshoot typical SQL errors (syntax issues, constraint violations) and test queries in a dedicated environment before making live changes. They can handle basic ETL-related tasks involving single data sources, maintain self-contained migrations, and handle small data transformations. While not fully versed in advanced performance tuning or broad concurrency management, they exhibit a solid foundation to grow toward more sophisticated SQL competencies over time. They still rely on guidance for advanced indexing strategies and concurrency configurations but have enough exposure to ensure basic reliability and correctness in standard SQL operations.
